facts.text: Ronkonkoma reflects the area’s Indigenous history, and the lake long served as an important landmark and shared gathering place.
Lake Ronkonkoma, the largest lake on Long Island, sits in a kettle hole that reaches about 65 feet deep at its southeastern side.
Because it’s a groundwater lake with no surface outlet, its water level has risen over five feet since the 1960s low, reaching a high in 2007.
Ronkonkoma Station, the eastern terminus of the Long Island Rail Road’s electric service, anchors the community and has driven decades of transit‑oriented redevelopment.
The land around Lake Ronkonkoma originally belonged to the Setauket, Unkechaug, Nissequogues, and Secatogs tribes before European settlement.
The $650 million Station Yards project, now called Station Yards, aims to spur economic growth beside the LIRR station and the lake’s border.
